questions.question: Explain the structure of an ecosystem with reference to its biotic and abiotic components.
keyPoints: Biotic components include all living organisms (producers, consumers, decomposers)
Abiotic components include non-living factors (sunlight, temperature, water, soil, pH)
Both components interact and are interdependent for ecosystem functioning

questions.question: Distinguish between a food chain and a food web.
keyPoints: Food chain is a linear sequence of organisms
Food web is an interconnected network of multiple food chains
Food webs show more complex feeding relationships and are more realistic

questions.question: Describe the 10% law of energy transfer in ecosystems.
keyPoints: Only 10% of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next
90% of energy is lost as heat through respiration and metabolic activities
This explains why food chains are limited to 4-5 trophic levels

questions.question: List and explain the four trophic levels in an ecosystem.
keyPoints: 1st Trophic Level - Producers (autotrophs)
2nd Trophic Level - Primary Consumers (herbivores)
3rd Trophic Level - Secondary Consumers (carnivores)
4th Trophic Level - Tertiary Consumers (top predators)

questions.question: What is the role of decomposers in maintaining ecosystem balance?
keyPoints: Decomposers break down dead organic matter
They release nutrients back into soil and water
They complete the nutrient cycling process
Without decomposers, nutrients would be locked in dead matter

questions.question: Explain the concept of an ecological pyramid with an example.
keyPoints: Ecological pyramid shows hierarchical organization of ecosystems
Shows energy/biomass/number at each trophic level
Shaped like a pyramid - broad base (producers), narrow top (apex predators)
Example: Grass (10000 J) → Grasshopper (1000 J) → Frog (100 J) → Snake (10 J)

questions.question: Explain the structure of an ecosystem under the following headings: (a) Biotic components and their categories (b) Abiotic components (c) Energy flow and trophic levels.
keyPoints: Biotic components: Producers (autotrophs), Consumers (herbivores, carnivores, omnivores), Decomposers
Abiotic components: Sunlight, temperature, water, soil, pH, minerals, atmosphere
Energy flow: Unidirectional, follows 10% law, through food chain/web
Trophic levels: Four levels - producers, primary, secondary, tertiary consumers
Examples for each category with proper explanation

questions.question: Describe the functional components of an ecosystem. How does energy flow differ from nutrient cycling in an ecosystem?
keyPoints: Functional components: Energy flow, nutrient cycling, ecosystem dynamics
Energy flow: Unidirectional, not recycled, follows 10% law, begins from sunlight
Nutrient cycling: Cyclic, nutrients reused, biogeochemical cycles (C, N, P)
Energy enters as sunlight, exits as heat; nutrients circulate repeatedly
Comparison table or detailed explanation of differences

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the different types of ecological pyramids. Why are most ecological pyramids upright?
keyPoints: Types: Pyramid of numbers, Pyramid of biomass, Pyramid of energy
Upright pyramid: Energy decreases at each level due to 10% law, biomass decreases, numbers may vary
Shows progressive reduction in energy, biomass from producers to apex predators
Explains limitation on food chain length
Diagram with labeled trophic levels

questions.question: Explain the importance of understanding ecosystem structure for engineering students in terms of environmental sustainability and resource management.
keyPoints: Environmental sustainability: Understanding carrying capacity, impact assessment, pollution control
Resource management: Renewable vs non-renewable resources, sustainable development
Engineering applications: Environmental impact assessment, waste management, green technology
Climate change: Carbon footprint, ecosystem services
Biodiversity conservation: Industrial ecology, sustainable design
